title: Ressentiment: The Psychology of Inverse Morality

source.original: On the Genealogy of Morality — Friedrich Nietzsche / Atlas Shrugged — Ayn Rand

body_html:
Nietzsche's most dangerous idea wasn't the Übermensch or the death of God. It was ressentiment — the psychological mechanism by which the weak don't just fail to create but redefine creation itself as immoral.

In the Genealogy of Morality (1887), Nietzsche traces how "slave morality" emerges not from genuine ethical insight but from inverted envy. The strong act. The weak react — and their reaction is to declare the strong's virtues as vices. Strength becomes "brutality." Ambition becomes "greed." Excellence becomes "privilege." The entire moral framework gets flipped not to produce justice but to protect the ego of those who chose not to compete.

James Taggart is ressentiment made flesh. He never says "I can't build a better railroad." He says "Building railroads for profit is selfish and wrong." The moral inversion is total. Dagny's competence isn't just threatening — it's reframed as morally inferior to his altruistic fumbling. Every time James invokes "the public good" or "social responsibility," he's executing the same move Nietzsche identified: turning his weakness into a virtue and her strength into a sin.

The mechanism is surgical. Ressentiment doesn't say "I failed." It says "Success is defined wrong." It doesn't say "I'm mediocre." It says "Excellence is elitist." It doesn't say "I chose comfort over greatness." It says "The pursuit of greatness is a pathology." The framework can absorb any achievement and reprocess it as evidence of moral failure.

This is why James can't be argued out of his position — it's not a position, it's a defense mechanism. Every piece of evidence that Dagny is right reinforces his need to believe she's wrong. The better she performs, the more "selfish" she must be. The more Rearden produces, the more "exploitative" his methods become. Ressentiment is an unfalsifiable worldview because it feeds on the very evidence that should disprove it.

Nietzsche saw this pattern in Christianity's revaluation of Roman virtues. Rand saw it in collectivism's revaluation of industrial achievement. Today it shows up whenever someone frames builders as extractors, innovators as disruptors-of-stability, or wealth creators as wealth hoarders. The vocabulary rotates. The psychology is identical.

The antidote isn't argument — ressentiment is immune to argument. The antidote is what Galt does: refuse to participate in a moral framework designed to punish you for being good at what you do. Stop apologizing for excellence. The Last Man's blink is contagious, but only if you look him in the eye and flinch first.
